1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
347	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
349	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
350	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
351	    lem:ratHilb_bump}
352	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
353	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
354	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
355	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
356	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
357	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
358	  %  function
359	  %  $$
360	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
361	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
362	  %  $$
363	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
364	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
365	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
366	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
367	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
368	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
369	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
370	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
371	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
372	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
373	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
374	  such that for all \(n > N\)
375	  \[
376	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
377	  \]
378	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
379	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
380	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
381	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
382	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
383	
384	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
385	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
386	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
387	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
388	  the production of the rational series from the module.
389	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality infrastructure (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
390	  %   and axiom-clean. The present theorem is not yet a Lean declaration: it is blocked on a
391	  %   genuine Mathlib gap. Mathlib at the pinned commit carries no packaged graded-module quotient
392	  %   \(M/xM\), graded kernel of a degree-shifting endomorphism, or regrading over \(R/(x)\) with
393	  %   finite-module transfer. Closing the inductive reduction requires building that
394	  %   graded-quotient/kernel/regrading API, a substantial sub-build.
395	\end{lemma}
396	
397	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
398	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
399	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
400	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
401	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
402	%  following short exact sequence
403	%  $$
404	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
405	%  $$
406	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
407	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
408	\begin{proof}
409	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
410	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
411	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition,
412	    lem:graded_quotient_decomposition, lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing,
413	    lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib, lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient,
414	    lem:graded_finite_transfer, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
415	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
416	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
417	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
418	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
419	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
420	
421	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
422	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
423	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
424	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
425	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
426	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). Then \(R\) is a graded
427	  quotient of a polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) with the \(x_i\) of
428	  degree \(1\); we induct on the minimal number \(r\) of degree-one generators.
429	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
430	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
431	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
432	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
433	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the G-assembly: degree-1 generation gives
434	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
435	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
436	
437	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} Here \(R = \kappa\) and \(M\) is a finitely
438	  generated \(\kappa\)-module, hence finite-dimensional, so \(M_n = 0\) and
439	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\). The Hilbert series
440	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is therefore a polynomial; taking \(d = 0\)
441	  and \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) the polynomial with these coefficients gives
442	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{0}\bigr)\) for all \(n\).
443	
444	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(x = x_r \in R_1\) be one of a minimal set of
445	  degree-one generators. The quotient ring \(R/(x)\) is itself a graded quotient
446	  of \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_{r-1}]\), hence an \((r-1)\)-generated graded
447	  \(\kappa\)-algebra to which the induction hypothesis applies; that \(R/(x)\) is a
448	  graded ring with degree-zero part \(\kappa\) is the content of \(G_3\)
449	  (\cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}), using that \((x)\) is homogeneous
450	  (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}).
451	
452	  \emph{Manufacturing \(K\) and \(C\) as graded \(R/(x)\)-modules.} Put
453	  \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\) and \(C = M/xM\). By \(G_1\)
454	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) \(K\) is an internally
455	  graded \(R\)-module with components \(K_n = \ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\), and by
456	  \(G_2\) (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}) \(C = M/xM\)
457	  is an internally graded \(R\)-module with components
458	  \(C_n = M_n/x M_{n-1}\). Both are annihilated by \(x\), so by \(G_4\)
459	  (\cref{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient}) their action descends to \(R/(x)\) with
460	  the same component families, and by \(G_5\)
461	  (\cref{lem:graded_finite_transfer}) they are finitely generated graded
462	  \(R/(x)\)-modules (\(C\) a quotient of the finitely generated \(M\), \(K\) a
463	  submodule of \(M\) over the Noetherian \(R\)). Writing
464	  \(h_M(n) = \dim_\kappa M_n\), \(h_C(n) = \dim_\kappa C_n\),
465	  \(h_K(n) = \dim_\kappa K_n\), the induction hypothesis applied to the
466	  \(R/(x)\)-modules \(C\) and \(K\) yields a common order \(d\) (after
467	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} raises the lower of the two orders) with
468	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
469	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}).
470	
471	  \emph{The difference identity.} Multiplication by \(x\) is the \(\kappa\)-linear
472	  map \(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}\); applying the degreewise rank--nullity identity
473	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) with \(V = M_n\),
474	  \(W = M_{n+1}\) gives, for every \(n\),
475	  \[
476	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
478	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n
479	      \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n),
480	  \]
481	  using the identifications \(\ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = K_n\) and
482	  \(M_{n+1}/x M_n = C_{n+1}\) from \(G_1\), \(G_2\). This is exactly the
483	  \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis of the inductive-step engine.
484	
485	  \emph{Conclusion.} Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\),
486	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\) and the difference identity into
487	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\): there are
488	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d+1 \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
489	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d+1)}\bigr)\) for all
490	  \(n > N\), which is the claim (with pole order \(d+1 \le r\)).
491	\end{proof}
492	
493	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
494	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
495	
496	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
497	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
498	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
499	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
500	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
501	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
502	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
503	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
504	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
505	
506	\begin{lemma}\leanok
507	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
508	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
510	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
511	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
512	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
513	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
514	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
515	  \(F\):
516	  \[
517	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
518	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
519	  \]
520	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
521	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
522	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
523	  the Cauchy product.
524	\end{lemma}
525	
526	\begin{lemma}\leanok
527	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
528	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
529	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
530	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
531	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
532	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
533	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
534	  \[
535	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
536	  \]
537	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
538	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
539	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
540	  \[
541	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
542	  \]
543	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
544	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
545	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
546	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
547	\end{lemma}
548	
549	\begin{proof}
550	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
551	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
552	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
553	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
554	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
555	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
556	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
557	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
558	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
559	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
560	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
561	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
562	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
563	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
564	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
565	\end{proof}
566	
567	\begin{definition}\leanok
568	[Rational Hilbert function]
569	  \label{def:ratHilb}
570	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
571	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
572	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
573	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
574	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
575	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
576	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
577	  \[
578	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
579	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
580	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
581	  \]
582	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
583	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
584	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
585	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
586	\end{definition}
587	
588	\begin{lemma}\leanok
589	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
590	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
591	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
592	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
593	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
594	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
595	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
596	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
597	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
598	\end{lemma}
599	
600	\begin{lemma}\leanok
601	[Raising the pole order]
602	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
603	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
604	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
605	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
606	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
607	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
608	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
609	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
610	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
611	\end{lemma}
612	
613	\begin{lemma}\leanok
614	[Closure under difference]
615	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
616	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
617	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
618	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
619	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
620	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
621	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
622	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
623	\end{lemma}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}\leanok
626	[Closure under right shift]
627	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
628	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
629	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
630	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
631	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
632	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
633	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
634	  Lean.
635	\end{lemma}
636	
637	\begin{lemma}\leanok
638	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
639	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
641	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
642	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
643	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
644	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
645	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
646	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
647	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
648	\end{lemma}
649	
650	\begin{proof}
651	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
652	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
653	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
654	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
655	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
656	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
657	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
658	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
659	\end{proof}
660	
661	\begin{lemma}\leanok
662	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
663	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
664	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
665	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
666	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
667	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
668	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
669	  \[
670	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
671	  \]
672	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
673	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
674	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
675	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
676	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
677	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
678	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
679	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
680	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
681	\end{lemma}
682	
683	\begin{proof}
684	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
685	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
686	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
687	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
688	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
689	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
690	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
691	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
692	\end{proof}
693	
694	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
695	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
697	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
698	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
699	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
700	  % Polynomials" (read from
701	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
702	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
703	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
704	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
705	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
706	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
707	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
708	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
709	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
710	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
711	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
712	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
713	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
714	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
715	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
716	  \[
717	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
718	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
719	  \]
720	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
721	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
722	\end{theorem}
723	
724	\begin{proof}
725	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
726	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
727	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
728	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
729	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
730	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
731	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
732	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
733	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
734	  function
735	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
736	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
737	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
738	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
739	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
740	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
741	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
742	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
743	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
744	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
745	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
746	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
747	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
748	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
749	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
750	\end{proof}
751	
752	\subsection{The graded-module wrapper for the Stacks~00K1 inductive step}
753	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
754	
755	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
756	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
757	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction: to apply the
758	inductive hypothesis to the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
759	element, those two modules must be presented as finitely generated graded modules
760	over the smaller ring \(R/(x)\). This subsection records the small wrapper that
761	manufactures that presentation. It is a thin layer over existing Mathlib scaffold:
762	Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
763	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the quotient \(M/xM\) as a named
764	object (\cref{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of homogeneous
765	elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
766	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
767	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib});
768	what is missing -- and supplied here -- is the \emph{induced grading} on the
769	derived objects (a homogeneous submodule, a homogeneous quotient module, the
770	quotient ring \(R/(x)\)) together with the regrading and finite-module transfer
771	over \(R/(x)\). Each block supplies an internal direct sum decomposition of the
772	relevant module together with the associated graded scalar multiplication.
773	
774	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
775	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
776	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a

13	For the graded-module side of the Stacks 00K1 inductive step (graded Hilbert–Serre
14	rationality, Lean target `AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational`):
15	(1) does Mathlib already supply the graded quotient `M/xM`, graded kernel
16	`K = ker(x⋆)`, regrading over `R/(x)`, and graded `Module.Finite` transfer; (2) what
17	is the Mathlib-aligned shape for these defs; (3) is there a route that avoids the
18	graded quotient/kernel entirely and works only with the degreewise `finrank`
19	identity?

21	## Project artifact(s)
22	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:425-612` — the `IsRatHilb` power-series
23	  toolkit (`IsRatHilb`, `.ofEventuallyZero`, `.bump`, `.sub`, `.shiftRight`,
24	  `.antidiff`, `.ofDiffEq`). DONE and axiom-clean. `ofDiffEq` is the inductive-step

68	## Decisions identified
69	
70	### Decision D1: graded quotient `C = M/xM` carrying a `DirectSum.Decomposition`
71	- **Mathlib idiom**: none ships, but the *shape* to mirror is `HomogeneousIdeal` /
72	  `GradedRing`: a grading is an **unbundled `DirectSum.Decomposition` instance** on a
73	  family `ℕ → Submodule …`. `xM` is a homogeneous submodule (image of mul-by-`x`,
74	  `x` homogeneous via `SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul`); the quotient's
75	  components are `(ℳ n).map (Submodule.mkQ (xM))`.
76	- **Gap**: divergent-and-missing → must build. General lemma G2 below.
77	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.
78	
79	### Decision D2: graded kernel `K = ker(x⋆ : M → M(1))`
80	- `K` **is** a `HomogeneousSubmodule 𝒜 ℳ` — reuse Mathlib's scaffold. `K = LinearMap.ker`
81	  of mul-by-`x`; an element lies in `K` iff each homogeneous component does (`x·mₙ ∈ ℳ_{n+1}`
82	  are independent), so `Submodule.IsHomogeneous` holds. The induced grading
83	  (`fun n => Kₙ := K ⊓ ℳ n` as a submodule of `M`) needs a `DirectSum.Decomposition`,
84	  which the scaffold does **not** provide. General lemma G1 below.
85	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.
86	
87	### Decision D3: regrading — `C`, `K` as graded modules over `R/(x)`
88	- `(x)` is a `HomogeneousIdeal` (`Ideal.homogeneous_span`). Two options:
89	  - **D3a (quotient ring, mirrors Stacks + blueprint)**: build `GradedRing` on `R ⧸ (x)`
90	    (components `(𝒜 n).map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (x))`), then `SetLike.GradedSMul` for the
91	    `R/(x)`-action on `C`, `K`. Needs the missing graded-quotient-ring construction (G3).
92	  - **D3b (graded subring, avoids the quotient ring)**: restrict scalars to the graded
93	    κ-subalgebra `R' = κ[x₁,…,x_{r−1}] ⊆ R`. Since `x_r` annihilates `C`, `K`, they are
94	    `R'`-modules with the **same** grading. Avoids quotient-ring grading but needs a
95	    `GradedRing` instance on a homogeneous subsemiring/subalgebra (also missing) +
96	    `RestrictScalars` graded compatibility.
97	- Both need a "grading on a derived ring" construction Mathlib lacks; D3a is closer to
98	  the existing `HomogeneousIdeal` API and the blueprint. **Recommend D3a.**
99	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.
100	
101	### Decision D4: `Module.Finite` transfer (graded, over `R/(x)`)
102	- `C`, `K` are f.g. over `R` (kernel/quotient of f.g. over Noetherian `R`) and
103	  annihilated by `(x)`; `Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective` (along `R ↠ R/(x)`)
104	  is the engine. Thin glue, no new theory — but it is glue that must be written.
105	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (low effort).
106	
107	### Decision D5: degreewise SES / `finrank` identity (the Q3 "avoidance" item)
108	- `dim Mₙ₊₁ − dim Mₙ = dim Cₙ₊₁ − dim Kₙ` is **pure linear algebra** on the κ-linear map
109	  `xₙ : Mₙ → Mₙ₊₁`: rank–nullity (`LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker`) gives
110	  `dim Kₙ = dim Mₙ − rk xₙ` and `dim(Mₙ₊₁/im xₙ) = dim Mₙ₊₁ − rk xₙ`; subtract. **No graded
111	  object is needed for this identity.** This is exactly the `hdiff` hypothesis of
112	  `IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq` — and it was already the trivial input.
113	- **Verdict**: PROCEED (build with pure linear algebra; needs no graded API).
114	
115	### Q3 — does the avoidance route collapse the sub-build? NO.
116	The degreewise identity (D5) genuinely avoids graded objects, but it is not the
117	obstruction. `IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq` *also* demands `hC' : IsRatHilb hC d` and
118	`hK' : IsRatHilb hK d`. The **only** source of those is the inductive hypothesis applied
119	to `C` and `K`, and the IH quantifies over **f.g. graded modules over an `(r−1)`-generated
120	ring**. To instantiate it, `C` and `K` must be presented as such objects — i.e. with
121	`DirectSum.Decomposition` (D1, D2), an `R/(x)`- or `R'`-module structure (D3), and
122	`Module.Finite` (D4). No reformulation feeds the IH "abstract vector-space families":
123	the recursion's payload *is* the graded structure. Free-resolution and graded-dévissage
124	reformulations exist but are strictly **harder** (Hilbert syzygy / graded primes — both
125	also absent from Mathlib). So Q3 removes D5 from the graded build (good, do it) but leaves
126	D1–D4 required.
127	
128	## Recommendation
129	
130	Build a small **graded-module-grading library** (genuine Mathlib gap-fill, all as
131	unbundled `DirectSum.Decomposition`/`SetLike.GradedSMul` instances mirroring
132	`HomogeneousIdeal`/`GradedRing`), then assemble Stacks 00K1 on top. Concrete plan:
133	
134	- **G1 — grading on a homogeneous submodule.** For `p : Submodule A M` with
135	  `p.IsHomogeneous ℳ`, produce `DirectSum.Decomposition (fun n => (p ⊓ ℳ n) as Submodule)`
136	  + `SetLike.GradedSMul 𝒜 (…)`. Gives `K` (D2) and `xM` graded. Generalises cleanly →
137	  PR-worthy. Mirror file: `RingTheory/GradedAlgebra/Homogeneous/Submodule.lean`.
138	- **G2 — grading on the quotient by a homogeneous submodule.** For `p.IsHomogeneous ℳ`,
139	  `DirectSum.Decomposition` on `M ⧸ p` with components `(ℳ n).map (p.mkQ)`. Gives `C` (D1).
140	  Same file. Proof spine: `M = ⨁ ℳ n` internal, `p = ⨁ (p ⊓ ℳ n)` (from G1), so
141	  `M/p = ⨁ ℳ n/(p ⊓ ℳ n)`.
142	- **G3 — grading on `R ⧸ I` for homogeneous `I`** (`GradedRing` instance) — the missing
143	  graded-quotient-**ring**; specialise to `I = (x)` for D3a. (Alt: D3b graded-subalgebra
144	  `GradedRing` + `RestrictScalars`.)
145	- **G4 — regrade `C`, `K` over `R/(x)`**: `SetLike.GradedSMul (R/(x))-grading (component
146	  family)`, reusing G1/G2 component families with the `R/(x)`-action.
147	- **G5 — `Module.Finite` transfer** via `Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective`
148	  (`R ↠ R/(x)`), using annihilation by `(x)`.
149	- **Assembly**: degreewise SES + identity via rank–nullity (D5, no graded API);
150	  induction on `r` (number of degree-1 generators) with base `R = κ`; inductive step feeds
151	  `hC', hK'` (from IH on `C`, `K` over `R/(x)`, via G1–G5) and `hdiff` (D5) into the
152	  existing `IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq` + `IsRatHilb.bump`.
153	
154	G1 and G2 are the load-bearing, reusable, PR-quality core; G3 is the heaviest single
155	piece. Order the next-iter mathlib-build lane G1 → G2 → G5 → G3 → G4 → assembly, since
156	G1/G2/G5 unblock the most with the least risk, and the degreewise identity can be landed
157	independently at any time.

781	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
782	
783	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
784	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
785	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
786	  \mathlibok
787	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
788	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
789	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
790	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
791	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
792	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
793	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
794	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure, but does \emph{not}
795	  supply the induced grading on \(p\) itself
796	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}).
797	\end{lemma}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}[The quotient module \(M/xM\)]
800	  \label{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}
801	  \lean{QuotSMulTop}
802	  \mathlibok
803	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.QuotSMulTop}).}
804	  For a commutative ring \(R\), an element \(x \in R\) and an \(R\)-module \(M\),
805	  Mathlib provides the cokernel of multiplication by \(x\) on \(M\),
806	  \[
807	    M/xM,
808	  \]
809	  as a named construction. It is the object \(C = M/xM\) whose induced grading is
810	  supplied by \cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}.
811	\end{lemma}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
814	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
815	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
816	  \mathlibok
817	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
818	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
819	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
820	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
821	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
822	  is homogeneous; this is the input that makes \(R/(x)\) a graded ring
823	  (\cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}).
824	\end{lemma}
825	
826	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
827	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
828	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
829	  \mathlibok
830	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
831	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
832	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
833	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
834	  \[
835	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
836	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
837	  \]
838	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
839	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
840	\end{lemma}
841	
842	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
843	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
844	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
845	  \mathlibok
846	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
847	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
848	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
849	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
850	  surjection \(R \twoheadrightarrow R/(x)\), this transfers finite generation of
851	  \(C\) and \(K\) from \(R\) to \(R/(x)\)
852	  (\cref{lem:graded_finite_transfer}).
853	\end{lemma}
854	
855	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
856	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
857	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
858	  \mathlibok
859	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
860	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)
861	  via a graded scalar multiplication. If \(a \in R\) is homogeneous of degree
862	  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has
863	  \(a \cdot m \in M_{i+n}\). This is the degree-shifting property that makes \(xM\)
864	  a homogeneous submodule (with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\)) and that
865	  feeds the induced gradings \(G_1\)--\(G_4\).
866	\end{lemma}
867	
868	\subsubsection*{Induced gradings on the derived objects}
869	
870	\begin{lemma}[$G_1$ -- grading on a homogeneous submodule]
871	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}
872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}
873	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
874	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(R\)-module and let
875	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
876	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
877	  \(n \mapsto p \cap M_n\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces is an internal direct sum
878	  decomposition of \(p\) -- i.e. \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) internally --
879	  and the \(R\)-action on \(M\) restricts to a graded scalar multiplication on
880	  \(p\). In particular the graded
881	  kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\), which is a homogeneous submodule (an element
882	  \(m = \sum_n m_n\) satisfies \(x m = 0\) iff each \(x m_n = 0\), because the
883	  \(x m_n\) lie in distinct degrees), inherits the grading
884	  \(K_n = K \cap M_n = \ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\).
885	\end{lemma}
886	
887	\begin{proof}
888	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
889	  The internal decomposition of \(M\) restricts to \(p\). For \(m \in p\), write
890	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) (the
891	  decomposition of \(M\)); homogeneity of \(p\) gives \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\),
892	  hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p \cap M_n\). This exhibits every element of \(p\)
893	  as a finite sum of elements of the subspaces \(p \cap M_n\), so the canonical map
894	  \(\bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n) \to p\) is surjective; it is injective because the
895	  \(M_n\) are independent in \(M\). Thus the family is an internal direct sum
896	  decomposition of \(p\). For the graded action: if \(a \in R_i\) and
897	  \(m \in p \cap M_n\), then \(a m \in M_{i+n}\) by
898	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib} and \(a m \in p\) because \(p\)
899	  is a submodule, so \(a m \in p \cap M_{i+n}\), which verifies the graded scalar
900	  multiplication condition. Finally \(K\) is homogeneous: the
901	  components \(x m_n \in M_{n+1}\) of \(x m\) lie in distinct degrees, so
902	  \(x m = 0\) forces each \(x m_n = 0\); thus \(K \cap M_n = \ker(x : M_n \to
903	  M_{n+1})\).
904	\end{proof}
905	
906	\begin{lemma}[$G_2$ -- grading on the quotient by a homogeneous submodule]
907	  \label{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}
908	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.quotient_decomposition}
909	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition, lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib,
910	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
911	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule. Then the quotient module
912	  \(M/p\) has an internal direct sum decomposition with degree-\(n\) component the
913	  image of \(M_n\) under the quotient map \(M \twoheadrightarrow M/p\), together
914	  with a compatible graded scalar multiplication. Specialising to \(p = xM\) gives
915	  the induced grading on \(C = M/xM\) (\cref{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}), with
916	  components \(C_n = M_n / x M_{n-1}\).
917	\end{lemma}
918	
919	\begin{proof}
920	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition, lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib,
921	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
922	  Surjectivity of the canonical map \(\bigoplus_n M_n/(p \cap M_n) \to M/p\) is
923	  immediate: the quotient map is surjective and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\), so every
924	  class \([m]\) is a finite sum of classes \([m_n]\) with \(m_n \in M_n\). For
925	  independence, suppose \(\sum_n [m_n] = 0\) in \(M/p\) with \(m_n \in M_n\). Then
926	  \(\sum_n m_n \in p\); by \(G_1\)
927	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) \(p =
928	  \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\), so taking degree-\(n\) components gives
929	  \(m_n \in p \cap M_n\), i.e. \([m_n] = 0\) for every \(n\). Hence the components
930	  \(M_n/(p \cap M_n)\) are independent and \(M/p \cong \bigoplus_n M_n/(p \cap M_n)\)
931	  is an internal direct sum decomposition. The graded action descends: for
932	  \(a \in R_i\) and a class \([m]\) with \(m \in M_n\), \(a \cdot [m] = [a m]\) and
933	  \(a m \in M_{i+n}\) by \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, so the
934	  action lands in component \(i+n\), giving the graded scalar multiplication.
935	  Taking \(p = xM\) -- homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\) by
936	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib} -- yields the grading on
937	  \(C = M/xM\).
938	\end{proof}
939	
940	\begin{lemma}[$G_3$ -- the quotient ring \(R/(x)\) is a graded ring]
941	  \label{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}
942	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.quotientRing_gradedRing}
943	  \uses{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib, lem:graded_quotient_decomposition,
944	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
945	  Let \(x \in R_1\), so that the principal ideal \((x)\) is homogeneous
946	  (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}). Then the quotient ring \(R/(x)\) is
947	  a graded ring with degree-\(n\) component the image of \(R_n\) under the
948	  quotient ring homomorphism \(R \twoheadrightarrow R/(x)\); that is,
949	  \(R/(x) = \bigoplus_n R_n/(R_n \cap (x))\) as a graded ring.
950	\end{lemma}
951	
952	\begin{proof}
953	  \uses{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib, lem:graded_quotient_decomposition,
954	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
955	  Viewing \(R\) as a graded module over itself and the homogeneous ideal \((x)\) as
956	  a homogeneous submodule, \(G_2\) (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition})
957	  supplies an internal direct sum decomposition of the underlying additive group of
958	  \(R/(x)\) with degree-\(n\) component the image of \(R_n\) under the quotient map.
959	  It remains to check the multiplicative compatibility making \(R/(x)\) a graded
960	  ring: the unit \(1\) is the image of \(1 \in R_0\), hence lies in the degree-zero
961	  component, and for \(\bar a \in (R/(x))_i\), \(\bar b \in (R/(x))_j\) -- images
962	  of \(a \in R_i\), \(b \in R_j\) -- the product \(\bar a \, \bar b = \overline{ab}\)
963	  is the image of \(a b \in R_{i+j}\) (because the homogeneous multiplication of
964	  \(R\) satisfies \(R_i R_j \subseteq R_{i+j}\)), so it lies in the
965	  degree-\((i+j)\) component. Since the quotient map is a ring homomorphism these
966	  conditions assemble into the graded ring structure on \(R/(x)\).
967	\end{proof}
968	
969	\begin{lemma}[$G_4$ -- regrading \(C\) and \(K\) over \(R/(x)\)]
970	  \label{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient}
971	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.regrade_over_quotient}
972	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition,
973	    lem:graded_quotient_decomposition, lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}
974	  Both \(C = M/xM\) and \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) are annihilated by \(x\):
975	  \(x \cdot C = 0\) since \(xM \subseteq xM\), and \(x \cdot K = 0\) by definition
976	  of the kernel. Hence the \(R\)-action on each factors through the surjection
977	  \(R \twoheadrightarrow R/(x)\), and the component families of \(G_1\)
978	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}, for \(K_n\)) and \(G_2\)
979	  (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}, for \(C_n\)) carry a graded scalar
980	  multiplication for the \(R/(x)\)-action of
981	  \cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}. Thus \(C\) and \(K\) are internally
982	  graded \(R/(x)\)-modules with the \emph{same} component families as over \(R\).
983	\end{lemma}
984	
985	\begin{proof}
986	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition,
987	    lem:graded_quotient_decomposition, lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}
988	  Annihilation by \(x\) makes the \(R\)-module structures on \(C\) and \(K\) descend
989	  to \(R/(x)\)-module structures: for \(\bar r \in R/(x)\) the action
990	  \(\bar r \cdot (-)\) is well-defined because any two lifts of \(\bar r\) differ by
991	  an element of \((x)\), which annihilates \(C\) and \(K\). The internal graded
992	  decompositions of \(C\) and \(K\) are unchanged (same component families from
993	  \(G_1\), \(G_2\)). For the graded action, the residue \(\bar a \in (R/(x))_i\)
994	  (image of \(a \in R_i\)) acts on a degree-\(n\) component by
995	  \(\bar a \cdot c = a \cdot c\), which lands in component \(i+n\) by the
996	  \(R\)-graded action of \(G_1\)/\(G_2\); independence of the value from the lift
997	  \(a\) again follows from annihilation by \((x)\). This gives the graded scalar
998	  multiplication for the \(R/(x)\)-grading of
999	  \cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}.
1000	\end{proof}
1001	
1002	\begin{lemma}[$G_5$ -- \(C\) and \(K\) are finitely generated over \(R/(x)\)]
1003	  \label{lem:graded_finite_transfer}
1004	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finite_over_quotient}
1005	  \uses{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient,
1006	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1007	  With \(R\) Noetherian, both \(C = M/xM\) and \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) are
1008	  finitely generated graded \(R/(x)\)-modules. Together with the gradings of
1009	  \(G_1\)--\(G_4\) this presents \(C\) and \(K\) as the finitely generated graded
1010	  \(R/(x)\)-modules to which the inductive hypothesis of
1011	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} applies.
1012	\end{lemma}
1013	
1014	\begin{proof}
1015	  \uses{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient,
1016	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1017	  As an \(R\)-module \(M\) is finitely generated; \(C = M/xM\) is a quotient of
1018	  \(M\), hence finitely generated over \(R\), and \(K \subseteq M\) is a submodule
1019	  of a finitely generated module over the Noetherian ring \(R\), hence also
1020	  finitely generated over \(R\). By \(G_4\)
1021	  (\cref{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient}) both are annihilated by \((x)\), so
1022	  their \(R\)-module structure factors through \(R \twoheadrightarrow R/(x)\).
1023	  \cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}, applied to this surjection,
1024	  transfers finite generation from \(R\) to \(R/(x)\): \(C\) and \(K\) are finitely
1025	  generated as \(R/(x)\)-modules. Combined with the graded \(R/(x)\)-module
1026	  structure of \(G_4\) they are finitely generated graded \(R/(x)\)-modules.
1027	\end{proof}
1028	
1029	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1030	
1031	\begin{lemma}[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1032	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1033	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1034	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1035	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1036	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1037	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1038	  \[
1039	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1040	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1041	  \]
1042	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\), \(V = M_n\),
1043	  \(W = M_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi = K_n\)
1044	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) and
1045	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = M_{n+1}/x M_n = C_{n+1}\)
1046	  (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}, since the degree-\((n+1)\) part of
1047	  \(xM\) is \(x M_n\)) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1048	  \[
1049	    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
1050	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1051	  \]
1052	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1053	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1054	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1055	\end{lemma}
1056	
1057	\begin{proof}
1058	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1059	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1060	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1061	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1062	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1063	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1064	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1065	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1066	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1067	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1068	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1069	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1070	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1071	  \(\ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = K_n\) and
1072	  \(M_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = C_{n+1}\) through the
1073	  gradings of \(G_1\) and \(G_2\), the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded
1074	  piece of the homogeneous submodule \(xM\) is exactly \(x M_n\)
1075	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1076	\end{proof}
1077	
1078	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1079	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1080	
1081	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1082	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1083	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1084	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1085	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1086	
1087	\begin{definition}
1088	
1089	\leanok
1090	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
1091	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
1092	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
1093	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1094	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
1095	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1096	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1097	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1098	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1099	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1100	  % Polynomials" (read from
1101	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1102	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1103	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1104	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1105	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1106	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1107	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1108	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
1109	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1110	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1111	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1112	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1113	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1114	  \(U \subseteq X\),
1115	  \[
1116	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1117	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
1118	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
1119	  \]
1120	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
1121	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
1122	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
1123	
1124	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
1125	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
1126	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
1127	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
1128	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
1129	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
1130	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
1131	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
1132	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
1133	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
1134	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
1135	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1136	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
1137	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
1138	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
1139	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1140	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
1141	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
1142	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
1143	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
1144	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
1145	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
1146	
1147	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
1148	\end{definition}
1149	
1150	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1151	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
1152	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
1153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
1154	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
1155	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
1156	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
1157	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
1158	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
1159	  \(U\):
1160	  \[
1161	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1162	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
1163	  \]
1164	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
1165	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
1166	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
1167	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
1168	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
1169	\end{lemma}
1170	
1171	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1172	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1173	  localization]
1174	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1175	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1176	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1177	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1178	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1179	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1180	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1181	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1182	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1183	  \[
1184	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1185	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1186	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1187	  \]
1188	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1189	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1190	
1191	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1192	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1193	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1194	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1195	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1196	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1197	\end{lemma}
1198	
1199	\begin{proof}
1200	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1201	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1202	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1203	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1204	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1205	
1206	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1207	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1208	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1209	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1210	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1211	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1212	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1213	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1214	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1215	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1216	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1217	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1218	\end{proof}

1221	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1222	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
